items.question: What is Base64 used for?
items.answer: Base64 is commonly used to transmit binary data over text-based protocols like email or HTTP, and to embed images in HTML or CSS.

items.question: Is Base64 encoding the same as encryption?
items.answer: No. Base64 is an encoding scheme, not encryption. It does not provide any security; it only transforms data into a text-safe format.

items.question: What is the difference between standard and URL-safe Base64?
items.answer: URL-safe Base64 replaces + with - and / with _ so the encoded string can be used in URLs and filenames without escaping.

items.question: Does Base64 increase file size?
items.answer: Yes. Base64 encoding increases data size by approximately 33% due to the way binary data is represented as text.

items.question: What is the difference between mean and median?
items.answer: The mean is the arithmetic average of all numbers. The median is the middle value when numbers are sorted. The median is less affected by outliers, making it a better measure of central tendency for skewed data.

items.question: Why is my standard deviation showing as zero?
items.answer: A standard deviation of zero means all numbers in your data set are identical. There is no variation among the values.

items.question: What does "no mode" mean?
items.answer: "No mode" means no number appears more than once in your data set. The mode is the most frequently occurring value, and if all values are unique, there is no mode.

items.question: Can I use decimal and negative numbers?
items.answer: Yes, the calculator supports decimal numbers, negative numbers, and zero. All statistics including mean, median, and standard deviation work correctly with these values.

items.question: What are RMS, geometric mean, and harmonic mean?
items.answer: RMS (Root Mean Square) is used in physics and signal processing. Geometric mean is useful for growth rates and requires all positive numbers. Harmonic mean is used for rates and averages of ratios.

items.question: How are outliers detected?
items.answer: Outliers are detected using the Tukey method. Any value below Q1 − 1.5×IQR or above Q3 + 1.5×IQR is flagged as a potential outlier, where IQR is the interquartile range.

items.question: Is MD5 still secure?
items.answer: No. MD5 is broken — it has known collision attacks. Use SHA-256 for security and SHA-3 for post-quantum readiness.

items.question: What is HMAC and when should I use it?
items.answer: HMAC is a keyed-hash message authentication code. Use it to verify both integrity and authenticity with a shared secret key.

items.question: Can hashes be reversed?
items.answer: No, hashes are one-way functions. However, unsalted hashes of common passwords can be looked up in rainbow tables.

items.question: What is the difference between SHA-2 and SHA-3?
items.answer: SHA-2 (SHA-256, SHA-512) uses a Merkle-Damgård structure. SHA-3 uses a sponge construction (Keccak) with a fundamentally different design.

items.question: Can I hash files with this tool?
items.answer: Yes. Switch to the File Upload tab and select any file. The hash is computed locally — your file never leaves your device.

items.question: How long should a password be?
items.answer: At least 12–16 characters is recommended. Longer passwords dramatically increase entropy and crack time.

items.question: What is password entropy?
items.answer: Entropy measures how hard a password is to guess. It is calculated as length × log₂(charset size). Higher entropy means stronger security.

items.question: Should I use a passphrase instead?
items.answer: Yes. A 4–5 word passphrase is often stronger than a random string and much easier to remember.

items.question: Are generated passwords truly random?
items.answer: This tool uses crypto.getRandomValues(), a cryptographically secure pseudo-random number generator (CSPRNG) built into every modern browser.

items.question: Can I customize the character set?

items.question: What's the difference between a password and a passphrase?
items.answer: A password is a random string of characters. A passphrase is a chain of random words that is easier to remember while remaining highly secure.

items.question: How is password entropy calculated?
items.answer: Entropy = length × log₂(charset size). For example, a 12‑char password with mixed case, digits, and symbols yields about 72 bits of entropy.

items.question: What do the crack times mean?
items.answer: They estimate how long an attacker with the hash would take via brute force. Offline GPU attacks can try 100+ billion guesses per second.

items.question: What makes a password strong?
items.answer: Length matters most (12+ characters). Combine uppercase, lowercase, digits, and symbols. Avoid dictionary words, keyboard patterns, and repeats.

items.question: Does this tool store or send my password?
items.answer: No. Everything runs locally in JavaScript — your password never leaves this browser tab.

items.question: What does "entropy" measure?
items.answer: Entropy measures unpredictability in bits. A password with 50 bits of entropy is 2⁵⁰ times harder to guess than one with 30 bits.

items.question: How do I interpret the crack time estimates?
items.answer: Crack times assume the attacker has the password hash. Online attacks are slower due to server throttling; offline GPU attacks can try billions of guesses per second.

items.question: What is a canonical tag?
items.answer: A canonical tag (rel="canonical") is an HTML element that helps prevent duplicate content issues by specifying the preferred version of a webpage to search engines.

items.question: When should I use a canonical tag?
items.answer: Use canonical tags when you have duplicate or similar content across multiple URLs, such as printer-friendly versions, session IDs, or multiple category paths.

items.question: Does a canonical tag guarantee ranking?
items.answer: No, the canonical tag is a hint, not a directive. Search engines may choose a different URL if they believe it better represents the content.

items.question: Can I use canonical tags across domains?
items.answer: Yes, cross-domain canonical tags tell search engines that a piece of content on another domain is the preferred version, useful for syndicated content.

items.question: Why are character counts important for SEO?
items.answer: Search engines truncate meta titles over 60 characters and meta descriptions over 160 characters. Keeping within limits ensures your snippets display fully in search results.

items.question: Does word count affect SEO ranking?
items.answer: While word count alone is not a direct ranking factor, comprehensive content that covers a topic in depth tends to perform better in search results.

items.question: What is keyword density?

items.question: Why is heading structure important for SEO?
items.answer: Proper heading structure (H1-H6) helps search engines understand the hierarchy and relevance of your content, improving readability and keyword relevance signals.

items.question: How many H1 tags should a page have?
items.answer: Best practice is to use one H1 tag per page that describes the main topic. Multiple H1s can confuse search engines about the primary subject.

items.question: What is a heading structure skip?
items.answer: A heading skip occurs when you jump from an H2 to an H4 without an H3 in between. This breaks the logical hierarchy and can confuse both users and search engines.

items.question: Can headings contain keywords?
items.answer: Yes, using relevant keywords in your headings helps search engines understand your content structure and can improve rankings for those terms.

items.question: What is readability in SEO?
items.answer: Readability measures how easy your content is to understand. Search engines favor content that is accessible to a wider audience, making readability an important SEO factor.

items.question: What is a good Flesch Reading Ease score?
items.answer: A score of 60-70 is considered standard and understandable by 13-15 year olds. Scores above 70 are fairly easy to read, while scores below 30 are very difficult.

items.question: How can I improve readability?
items.answer: Use shorter sentences, simpler words, active voice, bullet points, and clear headings. Break long paragraphs into smaller chunks.

items.question: Does readability affect rankings?
items.answer: Yes, search engines like Google consider user engagement signals. Content that is easy to read keeps users on the page longer, reducing bounce rates and improving rankings.

items.question: What is robots.txt?
items.answer: Robots.txt is a text file placed in a website's root directory that instructs search engine crawlers which pages or sections to crawl and which to ignore.

items.question: Can robots.txt block indexing?
items.answer: No, robots.txt only prevents crawling, not indexing. If external sites link to a blocked page, it may still appear in search results. Use noindex tags to prevent indexing.

items.question: Where should robots.txt be placed?
items.answer: Robots.txt must be placed in the root directory of your website, typically at example.com/robots.txt. It cannot be placed in subdirectories.

items.question: Can all crawlers be blocked?
items.answer: While you can set Disallow for all user-agents, malicious crawlers may ignore robots.txt. It is a voluntary protocol and not a security measure.
